name: Semaglutide (Wegovy/Ozempic)
whatItDoes: GLP-1 receptor agonist: increases satiety, slows gastric emptying, improves insulin secretion
dosageRange: Start 0.25mg/week for 4 weeks, then 0.5mg for 4 weeks, then 1.0mg, up to max 2.4mg/week. Inject same day each week. Titrate slowly to minimize nausea.
riskCautions: GI side effects; risk of pancreatitis; gallbladder disease; contraindicated in MTC/MEN2 history
keyEffects: Less hunger, slower digestion, better blood sugar; often improves BP and overall CV risk
bottomLine: Strongest data when used for its labeled indication under clinician guidance.
avoidIf: GI side effects; risk of pancreatitis; gallbladder disease; contraindicated in MTC/MEN2 history
cvNotes: Strong human outcomes data via diabetes/obesity trials; improves multiple CV risk factors (weight, glucose, BP).
drugInteractions: Caution with insulin/sulfonylureas (hypoglycemia risk); delays gastric emptying may affect absorption of oral meds; monitor for dehydration if on diuretics.

name: Tirzepatide (Zepbound/Mounjaro)
whatItDoes: Dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist: appetite reduction + improved glycemic control
dosageRange: Start 2.5mg/week for 4 weeks, titrate: 5mg→7.5mg→10mg→12.5mg→15mg. Increase every 4 weeks. Inject once weekly same day.
riskCautions: GI side effects; pancreatitis risk; gallbladder disease; thyroid C-cell tumor warning; hypoglycemia with insulin/sulfonylureas
keyEffects: Less hunger, slower digestion, better blood sugar; often improves BP and overall CV risk
bottomLine: Strongest data when used for its labeled indication under clinician guidance.
avoidIf: GI side effects; pancreatitis risk; gallbladder disease; thyroid C-cell tumor warning; hypoglycemia with insulin/sulfonylureas
cvNotes: Strong human outcomes data via diabetes/obesity trials; improves multiple CV risk factors (weight, glucose, BP).
drugInteractions: Caution with insulin/sulfonylureas (hypoglycemia risk); delays gastric emptying may affect absorption of oral meds; monitor for dehydration if on diuretics.

bottomLine: Strongest data when used for its labeled indication under clinician guidance.

name: Pramlintide (Symlin)
whatItDoes: Amylin analog: slows gastric emptying, suppresses glucagon, promotes satiety
commonUseExamples: Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes adjunct to mealtime insulin
dosageRange: T1D: start 15 mcg pre-meals; titrate; T2D: start 60 mcg pre-meals → 120 mcg
riskCautions: Hypoglycemia risk (requires insulin dose reduction); nausea; careful glucose monitoring
keyEffects: More satiety + slower digestion; improves post-meal blood sugar
bottomLine: Strongest data when used for its labeled indication under clinician guidance.
avoidIf: Hypoglycemia risk (requires insulin dose reduction); nausea; careful glucose monitoring
cvNotes: Improves post-meal glucose and satiety; indirect CV benefit via glycemic control (less direct outcomes data).
drugInteractions: Requires mealtime insulin dose reduction (hypoglycemia risk); delays gastric emptying—separate timing for oral meds that need rapid absorption.

name: Tesamorelin (Egrifta SV/WR)
whatItDoes: GHRH analog: stimulates endogenous GH; reduces visceral adipose tissue in HIV lipodystrophy
dosageRange: 1-2mg 1x daily in the evening subQ abdomen. FDA-approved for HIV lipodystrophy. Specifically targets visceral fat. Cycle: 12-26 weeks.
riskCautions: Glucose intolerance/diabetes risk; fluid retention; contraindications per PI; not indicated for weight loss
keyEffects: Signals the GH axis; can affect body composition, fluid retention, and glucose
bottomLine: Strongest data when used for its labeled indication under clinician guidance.
avoidIf: Glucose intolerance/diabetes risk; fluid retention; contraindications per PI; not indicated for weight loss
cvNotes: Improves visceral fat in a specific indication; indirect cardiometabolic benefits; monitor glucose.
drugInteractions: Can raise glucose; monitor if on diabetes meds; fluid retention may affect BP/CHF; avoid with active malignancy (per clinical guidance).

whatItDoes: GLP-2 analog increases intestinal absorption and mucosal growth

bottomLine: Strongest data when used for its labeled indication under clinician guidance.

name: Desmopressin (DDAVP)
whatItDoes: Vasopressin analog; reduces urine output; increases vWF release
commonUseExamples: Central diabetes insipidus; nocturnal enuresis; certain bleeding disorders
dosageRange: Indication-specific dosing
riskCautions: Hyponatremia/seizure risk; requires monitoring
keyEffects: Vasopressin analog; reduces urine output; increases vWF release
bottomLine: Strongest data when used for its labeled indication under clinician guidance.
avoidIf: Hyponatremia/seizure risk; requires monitoring
cvNotes: Limited direct cardiovascular evidence.
drugInteractions: Hyponatremia risk—caution with SSRIs/SNRIs, diuretics; fluid restriction and sodium monitoring.

name: Calcitonin
whatItDoes: Lowers serum calcium; affects bone turnover
dosageRange: Product-specific dosing
riskCautions: Cancer risk warning for some uses; nasal irritation
keyEffects: Lowers serum calcium; affects bone turnover
bottomLine: Strongest data when used for its labeled indication under clinician guidance.
avoidIf: Cancer risk warning for some uses; nasal irritation

name: Teriparatide (Forteo)
whatItDoes: PTH(1-34) analog: stimulates bone formation
commonUseExamples: Severe osteoporosis at high fracture risk
dosageRange: 20 mcg SC once daily
riskCautions: Hypercalcemia; orthostatic hypotension; osteosarcoma warning in animals; duration limits per guidelines
keyEffects: PTH(1-34) analog: stimulates bone formation
bottomLine: Strongest data when used for its labeled indication under clinician guidance.
avoidIf: Hypercalcemia; orthostatic hypotension; osteosarcoma warning in animals; duration limits per guidelines
cvNotes: No solid direct cardiovascular outcomes evidence; any benefit is indirect or unproven.
drugInteractions: Monitor calcium/vitamin D; caution with digoxin (hypercalcemia can increase toxicity risk).

name: Abaloparatide (Tymlos)
whatItDoes: PTHrP analog: anabolic bone agent
commonUseExamples: Osteoporosis in postmenopausal women at high risk
dosageRange: Product-specific dosing (PI)

whatItDoes: V1/V2 agonist; vasoconstriction and water retention
dosageRange: Hospital protocols only

keyEffects: V1/V2 agonist; vasoconstriction and water retention
bottomLine: Strongest data when used for its labeled indication under clinician guidance.

cvNotes: Clinically important in acute care settings; not a chronic 'heart health' therapy.

whatItDoes: Neuropeptide involved in pain and inflammation
dosageRange: N/A

name: Bivalirudin
whatItDoes: Direct thrombin inhibitor (peptide)
dosageRange: Hospital protocols only
riskCautions: Bleeding risk; medical supervision
keyEffects: Direct thrombin inhibitor (peptide)
bottomLine: Strongest data when used for its labeled indication under clinician guidance.

name: Eptifibatide
whatItDoes: GPIIb/IIIa inhibitor (peptide)
dosageRange: Hospital protocols only
riskCautions: Bleeding/thrombocytopenia risk
keyEffects: GPIIb/IIIa inhibitor (peptide)

name: Melanotan II (research)
whatItDoes: Melanocortin agonist (experimental)
dosageRange: Loading: 250-500mcg daily subQ for 2-3 weeks. Maintenance: 250-500mcg 1-2x/week. Start low — nausea is common at first. Use UV exposure to activate.
riskCautions: Not FDA-approved; nausea, BP effects, hyperpigmentation; melanoma concern; avoid
keyEffects: Melanocortin agonist (experimental)
bottomLine: Popular in research/compounding; human safety/benefit not well-established.
avoidIf: Avoid self-injection and unverified sources; discuss with a clinician.
cvNotes: No solid direct cardiovascular outcomes evidence; any benefit is indirect or unproven.
drugInteractions: BP effects possible; avoid with uncontrolled HTN; avoid if melanoma/skin cancer risk.

whatItDoes: Melanocortin pathway signaling
dosageRange: N/A

keyEffects: Melanocortin pathway signaling

dosageRange: N/A

name: Thymopentin (research/region-specific)
whatItDoes: Thymic peptide fragment studied for immune modulation
dosageRange: N/A
riskCautions: Regulatory status varies
keyEffects: Thymic peptide fragment studied for immune modulation
bottomLine: Popular in research/compounding; human safety/benefit not well-established.
avoidIf: Avoid self-injection and unverified sources; discuss with a clinician.
cvNotes: No solid direct cardiovascular outcomes evidence; any benefit is indirect or unproven.
drugInteractions: No well-established interaction profile for non-approved peptides; avoid combining multiple research compounds; discuss meds with clinician.

name: AOD-9604 (research)
whatItDoes: Modified HGH fragment (amino acids 177-191) that stimulates lipolysis and inhibits lipogenesis without affecting blood sugar or IGF-1 levels
commonUseExamples: Targeted fat loss; body recomposition without growth hormone side effects
dosageRange: 250–500 mcg/day subcutaneous injection, typically on empty stomach
riskCautions: Not FDA-approved; injection site reactions; limited long-term safety data; source quality concerns with compounded products
bestFor: Fat reduction without HGH-related metabolic side effects
keyEffects: Stimulates fat breakdown; inhibits fat formation; no effect on blood sugar, IGF-1, or growth; does not cause insulin resistance
bottomLine: Promising fat-loss peptide fragment with a favorable side-effect profile vs full HGH, but lacks robust human outcome data.
avoidIf: Pregnancy/breastfeeding; unverified peptide sources; avoid combining with other fat-loss agents without clinician oversight.
cvNotes: No direct cardiovascular outcomes data; theoretical benefit from fat reduction but unproven in trials.
drugInteractions: No well-established interaction profile; caution with insulin or other glucose-lowering agents; discuss with clinician.

name: IGF-1 LR3 (research)
whatItDoes: Long-acting analog of insulin-like growth factor 1 with extended half-life; potently stimulates muscle protein synthesis, satellite cell proliferation, and nutrient uptake
commonUseExamples: Muscle hypertrophy; recovery from muscle injury; bodybuilding
dosageRange: 20–50 mcg post-workout subcutaneous or intramuscular injection; cycles of 4–6 weeks
riskCautions: Hypoglycemia risk; potential tumor promotion with chronic use; joint pain; gut growth at high doses; not FDA-approved
bestFor: Anabolic muscle growth and recovery in advanced users
keyEffects: Powerful anabolic drive; increases muscle protein synthesis; enhances nutrient partitioning; promotes satellite cell activation; longer half-life than native IGF-1
bottomLine: Potent anabolic peptide with significant risks; should only be considered under strict medical supervision.
avoidIf: History of cancer or tumors; diabetes or hypoglycemia risk; pregnancy; avoid without clinician oversight.
cvNotes: No cardiovascular outcomes data; potential for cardiac hypertrophy with chronic use is a concern.
drugInteractions: Caution with insulin or sulfonylureas (severe hypoglycemia risk); may interact with growth hormone therapy; discuss all medications with clinician.

name: Follistatin 344 (research)
whatItDoes: Naturally occurring glycoprotein that binds and inhibits myostatin, the primary negative regulator of muscle growth, allowing enhanced muscle hypertrophy
commonUseExamples: Muscle growth beyond natural limits; countering age-related muscle loss; bodybuilding
dosageRange: 100 mcg/day subcutaneous injection for 10–30 day cycles
riskCautions: Not FDA-approved; limited human safety data; theoretical concerns about uncontrolled muscle growth, tendon strain, and reproductive effects
bestFor: Overcoming muscle growth plateaus via myostatin inhibition
keyEffects: Blocks myostatin signaling; promotes muscle fiber hypertrophy; may enhance fat loss indirectly through increased lean mass
bottomLine: Exciting myostatin-inhibition approach but human data is very limited; use only under medical supervision.
avoidIf: Pregnancy; history of hormone-sensitive conditions; unverified sources; avoid without medical guidance.
cvNotes: No cardiovascular outcomes data; theoretical benefit from increased lean mass but unproven.
drugInteractions: No well-established interaction profile; caution with anabolic agents; discuss with clinician.

name: Gonadorelin
whatItDoes: Synthetic GnRH (gonadotropin-releasing hormone) analog that stimulates the pituitary to release LH and FSH, supporting endogenous testosterone production
commonUseExamples: Maintaining fertility during TRT; testosterone support; diagnosing pituitary function; post-cycle therapy
dosageRange: 100–200 mcg subcutaneous injection 2x/week
riskCautions: Headache; flushing; injection site reactions; may cause hormonal fluctuations; requires monitoring of hormone levels
bestFor: Preserving testicular function and fertility while on testosterone replacement therapy
keyEffects: Stimulates LH and FSH release; maintains endogenous testosterone production; preserves fertility and testicular size
evidenceLevel: FDA-approved diagnostic agent; clinical use for fertility preservation
bottomLine: Well-established GnRH analog commonly used to preserve fertility during TRT; requires clinician monitoring.
avoidIf: Hormone-sensitive cancers; pregnancy; conditions worsened by gonadotropin stimulation.
cvNotes: No direct CV outcomes data; indirect benefit through testosterone optimization is possible but unproven.
drugInteractions: May interact with other hormonal therapies (TRT, hCG, clomiphene); monitor hormone levels closely when combining.

name: FOXO4-DRI (research)
whatItDoes: Cell-penetrating peptide that disrupts the FOXO4-p53 interaction in senescent cells, forcing them into apoptosis while sparing healthy cells
commonUseExamples: Clearing senescent (zombie) cells; anti-aging protocols; longevity research
dosageRange: 5–10 mg/day subcutaneous injection for short cycles (typically 3–5 days, repeated monthly)
riskCautions: Very early research stage; no human clinical trials; unknown long-term safety; extremely expensive; source quality critical
bestFor: Targeted removal of senescent cells for longevity and tissue rejuvenation
keyEffects: Selectively induces apoptosis in senescent cells; may restore tissue function; reduces inflammatory SASP factors; shown to restore fitness and organ function in aged mice
evidenceLevel: Early research (animal studies only; no human trials)
bottomLine: Groundbreaking senolytic concept but human evidence is absent; experimental use only under research supervision.
avoidIf: Immunocompromised states; active cancer treatment (unknown interactions); pregnancy; anyone not under direct research/medical supervision.
cvNotes: No cardiovascular data; theoretical benefit from reducing vascular senescent cell burden but entirely unproven in humans.
drugInteractions: No established interaction profile; avoid combining with other senolytics or immunosuppressants without guidance.
stacksWellWith: NAD+ (not a peptide, but commonly discussed)

name: SS-31 / Elamipretide (clinical trials)
whatItDoes: Mitochondrial-targeted tetrapeptide that concentrates in the inner mitochondrial membrane, stabilizing cardiolipin and improving electron transport chain efficiency and ATP production
commonUseExamples: Mitochondrial dysfunction; heart failure; Barth syndrome; age-related cellular energy decline
dosageRange: 40 mg/day subcutaneous injection (clinical trial dosing)
riskCautions: Investigational drug; injection site reactions; not commercially available outside trials; limited long-term data
bestFor: Restoring mitochondrial function in diseases of mitochondrial dysfunction
keyEffects: Stabilizes cardiolipin in inner mitochondrial membrane; improves ATP production; reduces reactive oxygen species; protects mitochondrial structure
evidenceLevel: Clinical trials (Phase 2/3 for Barth syndrome and heart failure)
bottomLine: Leading mitochondrial therapeutic in clinical development; promising for mitochondrial diseases and potentially aging.
avoidIf: Outside of clinical trial settings; pregnancy; unknown interactions with other mitochondrial-targeted agents.
cvNotes: Active clinical trials in heart failure show potential; cardiolipin stabilization directly relevant to cardiac mitochondrial function.
drugInteractions: Limited interaction data from trials; caution with other mitochondrial-targeted compounds; discuss with research clinician.
stacksWellWith: NAD+ (not a peptide, but commonly discussed)

name: Pinealon (bioregulator)
whatItDoes: Short bioregulator peptide (Glu-Asp-Arg) that targets pineal gland and brain tissue, supporting melatonin synthesis and neuroprotective gene expression
commonUseExamples: Sleep improvement; neuroprotection; age-related cognitive decline; circadian rhythm support
dosageRange: 10 mg/day oral or sublingual; typically in 10–20 day cycles
riskCautions: Limited human clinical data; bioregulator peptides are a niche category; source quality varies; not FDA-approved
bestFor: Supporting sleep quality and neuroprotection through pineal gland bioregulation
keyEffects: Supports melatonin production; neuroprotective effects on brain tissue; may improve sleep onset and quality; antioxidant properties in CNS
evidenceLevel: Bioregulator research (Russian peptide bioregulator tradition; limited Western clinical data)
bottomLine: Part of the Khavinson bioregulator peptide family; interesting but evidence is mostly from Russian literature and not widely replicated.
avoidIf: Pregnancy; autoimmune CNS conditions; combining with sedatives without medical guidance.
cvNotes: No cardiovascular outcomes data.
drugInteractions: May potentiate sedatives or sleep medications; caution with melatonin supplements; discuss with clinician.

name: 5-Amino-1MQ (research)
whatItDoes: Small molecule NNMT (nicotinamide N-methyltransferase) inhibitor that increases NAD+ levels in fat cells, boosting cellular metabolism and promoting fat oxidation
commonUseExamples: Fat loss; metabolic enhancement; NAD+ boosting; body recomposition
dosageRange: 50–100 mg oral daily; some protocols use up to 150 mg/day
riskCautions: Not FDA-approved; limited human data; potential GI discomfort; long-term effects on NNMT inhibition unknown; technically a small molecule, not a peptide
bestFor: Metabolic enhancement and fat loss through NAD+ pathway modulation
keyEffects: Inhibits NNMT enzyme; increases intracellular NAD+; enhances fat cell metabolism; may reduce fat mass while preserving muscle; activates SAM-dependent pathways
evidenceLevel: Research (preclinical/early human use; no FDA indication)
bottomLine: Promising NNMT inhibitor for metabolic health, but human evidence is early-stage; oral bioavailability is an advantage.
avoidIf: Liver or kidney disease; pregnancy; combining with other NAD+-modulating compounds without guidance.
cvNotes: Theoretical metabolic benefit through NAD+ increase and fat reduction, but no direct CV outcomes data.
drugInteractions: May interact with NAD+ precursors (NMN, NR); caution with other metabolic drugs; no established interaction profile.
stacksWellWith: NAD+ (not a peptide, but commonly discussed)

name: Mazdutide (research/clinical trials)
whatItDoes: Dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist that combines appetite suppression with glucagon-mediated energy expenditure increase and fat oxidation
dosageRange: Titrated dosing similar to other incretin therapies; Phase 3 trials use weekly subcutaneous injection (doses up to 9 mg)
riskCautions: GI side effects (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea); investigational drug; not yet approved; potential hepatotoxicity concerns
bestFor: Weight loss with enhanced energy expenditure via glucagon component
keyEffects: Reduces appetite via GLP-1 activity; increases energy expenditure and fat oxidation via glucagon activity; improves glycemic control; may reduce liver fat
bottomLine: Next-generation dual agonist with glucagon component for added metabolic benefit; advancing through late-stage trials.
avoidIf: GI conditions; pancreatitis history; thyroid C-cell tumor history (MTC/MEN2); pregnancy.
cvNotes: Potential CV benefit from weight loss and metabolic improvement; glucagon component may offer hepatic benefits; awaiting outcomes data.
drugInteractions: Caution with insulin/sulfonylureas (hypoglycemia risk); may affect absorption of oral medications due to delayed gastric emptying.

name: Survodutide (research/clinical trials)
whatItDoes: Dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist in late-stage clinical development for obesity and NASH/MASH, combining appetite suppression with glucagon-driven energy expenditure
commonUseExamples: Obesity; NASH/MASH (metabolic liver disease); Type 2 diabetes
dosageRange: Weekly subcutaneous injection; Phase 3 trial doses titrated up to 4.8–6.0 mg
riskCautions: GI side effects; nausea; investigational status; not commercially available; pancreatitis risk (class effect)
bestFor: Weight loss and liver fat reduction through dual incretin/glucagon mechanism
keyEffects: Appetite suppression via GLP-1; increased energy expenditure and hepatic fat oxidation via glucagon; significant weight loss in trials (~18-20%); liver fat reduction
evidenceLevel: Clinical trials (Phase 3 for obesity and NASH)
bottomLine: Strong Phase 3 data for both weight loss and liver disease; potential best-in-class for NASH if approved.
avoidIf: Pancreatitis history; MTC/MEN2 history; GI conditions; pregnancy.
cvNotes: Potential CV benefit through substantial weight loss and liver fat reduction; awaiting dedicated CV outcomes trials.
drugInteractions: Caution with insulin/sulfonylureas; may delay absorption of oral medications; monitor hepatic function.
stacksWellWith: NAD+ (not a peptide, but commonly discussed)

name: Adipotide (research)
whatItDoes: Peptidomimetic that targets PROHIBITIN on blood vessels supplying white adipose tissue, triggering apoptosis of the vascular endothelium feeding fat deposits
dosageRange: No established human dose; animal studies used 0.5–1 mg/kg; not suitable for self-administration
riskCautions: Significant renal toxicity observed in primate studies; dehydration; potential organ damage; NOT safe for human use outside controlled research
bestFor: Experimental research only; demonstrates proof-of-concept for vascular-targeted fat reduction
keyEffects: Destroys blood vessels feeding fat tissue; causes rapid fat cell death; significant weight loss in animal models; severe side effects including kidney damage
evidenceLevel: Research only (animal studies; significant toxicity concerns)
bottomLine: Interesting proof-of-concept but too toxic for human use in current form; renal toxicity is a serious limitation.
avoidIf: ALL human use outside strict research settings; kidney disease; dehydration risk; any self-administration.
cvNotes: No CV benefit data; significant renal and vascular toxicity concerns make this a poor candidate for clinical use.
drugInteractions: No established profile; significant nephrotoxicity means extreme caution with any renal-affecting drugs.

name: MGF (Mechano Growth Factor, research)
whatItDoes: Splice variant of IGF-1 produced locally in muscle tissue after mechanical stress; activates satellite cells for muscle repair and hypertrophy
commonUseExamples: Post-workout muscle repair; injury recovery; bodybuilding
dosageRange: 200 mcg intramuscular injection post-workout into target muscle; short half-life (~minutes)
riskCautions: Very short half-life limits practical use; not FDA-approved; injection site pain; limited human data; source quality concerns
bestFor: Localized muscle repair and satellite cell activation post-exercise
keyEffects: Activates muscle satellite cells; promotes local muscle repair; stimulates muscle stem cell proliferation; very short-acting
bottomLine: Physiologically interesting muscle repair peptide but impractical due to extremely short half-life; PEG-MGF may be more practical.
avoidIf: Cancer history; pregnancy; avoid without medical supervision.
cvNotes: No cardiovascular outcomes data.
drugInteractions: Caution with insulin (hypoglycemia); no well-established interaction profile; discuss with clinician.

name: PEG-MGF (research)
whatItDoes: PEGylated (polyethylene glycol-modified) version of Mechano Growth Factor with extended half-life, providing sustained satellite cell activation and muscle repair signaling
dosageRange: 200 mcg subcutaneous or intramuscular injection 2–3x/week
riskCautions: Not FDA-approved; limited human data; injection site reactions; potential for uncontrolled growth signaling
bestFor: Sustained muscle repair and growth with practical dosing frequency
keyEffects: Extended-release satellite cell activation; promotes muscle hypertrophy; systemic muscle repair signaling; longer half-life than native MGF
bottomLine: More practical than native MGF due to PEGylation extending half-life, but still lacks human clinical evidence.
avoidIf: Cancer history; pregnancy; avoid without medical supervision.
cvNotes: No cardiovascular outcomes data.
drugInteractions: Caution with insulin (hypoglycemia); no well-established interaction profile; discuss with clinician.

name: Thymulin (research)
whatItDoes: Thymic nonapeptide (requires zinc cofactor) that modulates T-cell differentiation, immune balance, and has anti-inflammatory properties; declines significantly with age
commonUseExamples: Immune system rejuvenation; age-related immune decline (immunosenescence); autoimmune modulation research
dosageRange: 1–5 mg subcutaneous injection weekly; cycle protocols vary
riskCautions: Not FDA-approved; limited human data outside of aging research; zinc status affects activity; source quality critical
bestFor: Restoring thymic immune function in age-related immune decline
keyEffects: Modulates T-cell maturation and differentiation; restores immune balance; anti-inflammatory properties; requires zinc for bioactivity
evidenceLevel: Research (preclinical and limited human aging research)
bottomLine: Interesting thymic peptide for immunosenescence research, but clinical evidence is limited.
avoidIf: Active autoimmune flares; immunosuppressive therapy; pregnancy; zinc deficiency (reduces efficacy).
cvNotes: No direct cardiovascular outcomes data; theoretical anti-inflammatory benefit but unproven.
drugInteractions: May interact with immunosuppressants or immunomodulators; zinc supplementation may be needed for activity; discuss with clinician.

name: Larazotide (clinical trials)
whatItDoes: Tight junction modulator that prevents zonulin-mediated opening of intestinal tight junctions, reducing intestinal permeability (leaky gut)
commonUseExamples: Celiac disease (adjunct to gluten-free diet); intestinal permeability disorders; leaky gut research
dosageRange: 0.5–1 mg oral capsule, taken before meals (clinical trial dosing)
riskCautions: Investigational (not yet FDA-approved); GI side effects possible; designed as adjunct to gluten-free diet, not a replacement
bestFor: Reducing intestinal permeability in celiac disease and related conditions
keyEffects: Blocks zonulin-mediated tight junction opening; reduces paracellular permeability; decreases gluten-related GI symptoms; acts locally in the gut lumen
bottomLine: Most advanced tight junction modulator in development; oral and locally-acting with promising celiac disease data.
avoidIf: Not a substitute for gluten-free diet in celiac disease; pregnancy; severe GI conditions without medical oversight.
cvNotes: No direct CV relevance; gut barrier function may have indirect systemic inflammatory implications.
drugInteractions: Oral administration means potential interaction with other oral medications; take separately from other gut-active drugs; discuss with GI specialist.

name: P21 (research)
whatItDoes: Synthetic peptide derived from CNTF (ciliary neurotrophic factor) that promotes neurogenesis in the hippocampus by inhibiting leukemia inhibitory factor signaling
commonUseExamples: Cognitive enhancement; neurogenesis promotion; Alzheimer's disease research; memory improvement
dosageRange: 700 mcg/day subcutaneous injection; typical cycles of 2–4 weeks
riskCautions: Not FDA-approved; very limited human data; injection site reactions; unknown long-term effects of exogenous neurogenesis stimulation
bestFor: Promoting new neuron growth in the hippocampus for cognitive enhancement
keyEffects: Stimulates hippocampal neurogenesis; enhances spatial memory and learning in animal models; increases BDNF expression; inhibits LIF signaling pathway
evidenceLevel: Research (animal studies; no human clinical trials)
bottomLine: Fascinating neurogenic peptide with strong animal data but no human trials; experimental use only.
avoidIf: History of brain tumors; seizure disorders; pregnancy; any use without research/medical supervision.
cvNotes: No cardiovascular data.
drugInteractions: No established interaction profile; caution with other neurotropic agents; discuss with clinician.

name: PE-22-28 (research)
whatItDoes: Synthetic peptide fragment derived from spadin that acts as a TREK-1 channel blocker, exhibiting antidepressant-like and cognitive-enhancing properties
commonUseExamples: Cognitive enhancement; antidepressant research; memory and learning improvement
dosageRange: Research dosing not well established for humans; animal studies use varying protocols
riskCautions: Very early research; no human clinical data; unknown safety profile; not FDA-approved; source quality concerns
bestFor: Cognitive enhancement and mood support through TREK-1 channel modulation
keyEffects: Blocks TREK-1 potassium channels; increases BDNF and neurogenesis; antidepressant-like effects in animal models; enhances synaptic plasticity
bottomLine: Interesting TREK-1 channel mechanism for cognition and mood, but extremely early-stage with no human evidence.
avoidIf: All self-administration; pregnancy; seizure disorders; combining with antidepressants without guidance.
cvNotes: No cardiovascular data.
drugInteractions: Theoretical interaction with antidepressants (SSRIs, SNRIs) via overlapping neurotropic mechanisms; avoid combining without medical guidance.

name: Cartalax (bioregulator)
whatItDoes: Short bioregulator peptide (Ala-Glu-Asp) targeting cartilage tissue, supporting chondrocyte function and cartilage matrix maintenance
commonUseExamples: Joint health; cartilage repair support; age-related joint degeneration; osteoarthritis research
dosageRange: 20 mg oral or sublingual per day in cycles of 10–30 days; repeat cycles as needed
riskCautions: Limited clinical data outside Russian bioregulator research; not FDA-approved; source quality varies
bestFor: Supporting cartilage health and joint function through bioregulatory peptide signaling
keyEffects: Supports chondrocyte gene expression; promotes cartilage matrix maintenance; may reduce age-related cartilage degeneration; bioregulatory action on cartilage tissue
evidenceLevel: Bioregulator research (Khavinson peptide bioregulator tradition; limited Western data)
bottomLine: Part of the Khavinson bioregulator peptide family targeting cartilage; limited but interesting preliminary data.
avoidIf: Pregnancy; autoimmune joint conditions without medical guidance; unverified sources.
cvNotes: No cardiovascular relevance.
drugInteractions: No well-established interactions; caution with other joint supplements or DMARDs; discuss with clinician.

name: SNAP-8 (Acetyl octapeptide-3)
whatItDoes: Topical peptide that mimics the N-terminal end of SNAP-25, inhibiting SNARE complex formation to reduce neuromuscular signaling at the skin surface, decreasing expression lines
commonUseExamples: Topical anti-wrinkle treatment; expression line reduction; Botox alternative for cosmetic use
dosageRange: Topical application; typically 3–10% concentration in serums or creams; applied 1–2x daily to target areas
riskCautions: Topical only (not injectable); effects are milder than Botox; individual results vary; allergic reactions possible; not FDA-approved as a drug
bestFor: Non-invasive reduction of expression wrinkles as a topical Botox alternative
keyEffects: Inhibits SNARE complex assembly; reduces neurotransmitter release at neuromuscular junction (topically); decreases muscle contraction intensity; smooths expression lines over time
evidenceLevel: Cosmetic research (in vitro and small human cosmetic studies)
bottomLine: Popular cosmetic peptide with a sound mechanism but much milder than injectable neuromodulators; good for those avoiding Botox.
avoidIf: Known skin allergies to peptide ingredients; do not inject; not a replacement for medical-grade neuromodulators.
cvNotes: No cardiovascular relevance; topical cosmetic use only.
drugInteractions: No systemic interactions expected with topical use; avoid combining with other active topicals (retinoids, acids) on same area simultaneously.
